Explorer.goal: Learn to read the prompt exactly, simulate small examples, and state the invariant before reaching for a pattern name.
graduation_criteria: Explains the problem in plain language without losing constraints.
Builds at least two worked examples before proposing an approach.
Produces a correct brute-force baseline and identifies what makes it slow.

Observer.goal: Spot how local state changes as the input is scanned, and recognize when memory can be compressed into pointers, windows, or counts.
graduation_criteria: Identifies the moving boundary or state variable that controls the scan.
Chooses between pointer movement, window expansion, and hash-based bookkeeping without prompting.
Explains why the chosen invariant eliminates repeated work.

Searcher.goal: Recognize monotonic structure, ordered representations, and cumulative transforms that convert repeated scans into faster queries.
graduation_criteria: Justifies when sorting first is worth the cost.
Writes a monotonic feasibility check for answer-space search.
Uses prefix transforms to convert repeated range work into O(1) or amortized constant checks.

State Builder.goal: Manipulate explicit program state with pointer structures, stacks, queues, and heaps without losing track of invariants.
graduation_criteria: Draws state transitions for linked structures before coding pointer updates.
Uses stacks, deques, or heaps only when the maintained ordering is explicit.
Can explain why each pushed or popped item will never be needed in its previous position again.

Recursive Thinker.goal: Decompose hierarchical structures into reusable subproblems and combine subtree results cleanly.
graduation_criteria: States the recursive contract before traversing.
Separates traversal order from business logic.
Uses ordered-tree or prefix-tree invariants to prune search work.

Navigator.goal: Model problems as graphs, pick the right traversal or connectivity tool, and reason about reachability before optimization.
graduation_criteria: Chooses between BFS, DFS, Union-Find, topological order, and shortest-path tools with a clear graph model.
Defines nodes, edges, and visited state explicitly.
Explains the difference between traversal correctness and path optimality.

Optimizer.goal: Build states and transitions deliberately, then compress or reorder them without breaking the recurrence.
graduation_criteria: Defines state, transition, and base cases before implementation.
Can explain why greedy or local reasoning fails on the same problem family.
Chooses an evaluation order that guarantees dependencies are already solved.

Inventor.goal: Generate possibilities, prune aggressively, and deploy proof-driven shortcuts in greedy, recursive, and arithmetic settings.
graduation_criteria: Defines the decision tree for a search problem before recursing.
Defends a greedy choice with an exchange argument, invariant, or contradiction.
Uses number or bit properties to replace simulation with direct reasoning.

Engineer.goal: Compose multiple data structures into durable systems, support online updates, and reason about API behavior under sustained use.
graduation_criteria: Chooses storage, indexing, and update rules as part of the solution, not as an afterthought.
Keeps interface guarantees and operational complexity aligned.
Can explain tradeoffs between simpler implementations and stronger asymptotic guarantees.
